summary: Joel Spolsky presents a simple 12-question yes/no test for evaluating the quality of a software development team, as a practical alternative to complex methodologies like SEMA. The test covers fundamentals like source control, one-step builds, daily builds, bug tracking, specs, quiet offices, good tools, testers, coding interviews, and hallway usability testing. He argues that most organizations score only 2 or 3 out of 12, while companies like Microsoft consistently score 12. Each question is explained with practical rationale and real-world anecdotes, including Microsoft Word's 'infinite defects methodology' disaster. The test is designed to be quick and actionable, giving teams and job candidates a fast way to assess engineering maturity.
key_points: A simple yes/no checklist can effectively measure software team quality without complex metrics, with scores below 10 indicating serious problems
Fixing bugs before writing new code is critical because bug-fixing costs grow exponentially over time and make schedules unreliable
Programmer productivity depends heavily on uninterrupted flow state, making quiet private offices far more productive than open-plan layouts
Specs, schedules, and process discipline aren't bureaucratic overhead — they prevent the kind of chaos that killed Netscape and plagued early Microsoft Word
Hiring programmers without having them write code is as absurd as hiring a magician without seeing tricks or a caterer without tasting food
money_quotes: A score of 12 is perfect, 11 is tolerable, but 10 or lower and you've got serious problems.
The schedule was merely a checklist of features waiting to be turned into bugs. In the post-mortem, this was referred to as 'infinite defects methodology'.
In general, the longer you wait before fixing a bug, the costlier (in time and money) it is to fix.
Writing specs is like flossing: everybody agrees that it's a good thing, but nobody does it.

If a coworker asks you a question, causing a 1 minute interruption, but this knocks you out of the zone badly enough that it takes you half an hour to get productive again, your overall productivity is in serious trouble.
Top notch development teams don't torture their programmers.
Would you hire a magician without asking them to show you some magic tricks? Of course not.

If your team doesn't have dedicated testers, at least one for every two or three programmers, you are either shipping buggy products, or you're wasting money by having $100/hour programmers do work that can be done by $30/hour testers.
key_insight: Software team quality can be reliably assessed with 12 simple yes/no questions covering engineering fundamentals, and most teams fail badly because they neglect basic practices like bug tracking, specs, and quiet working conditions.
